title: Diagnose a slow query by reading its execution plan
description: Capture EXPLAIN ANALYZE, find the dominant operator and the estimate-vs-actual row gap, and target the real bottleneck before changing SQL.
body: Steps: 1. Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on the actual query and parameters. 2. Find the operator consuming the most actual time (often a seq scan, nested loop, or sort/hash spill). 3. Compare estimated vs actual rows; a large gap means stale statistics or a non-sargable predicate misleading the planner. 4. Decide the fix: add/adjust an index, rewrite a non-sargable predicate (avoid functions on the indexed column), update statistics (ANALYZE), or restructure the join. 5. Re-run EXPLAIN ANALYZE and compare. Rollback point: before adding an index on a write-heavy table (it slows writes). Validation: the new plan removes the dominant scan/spill and total time drops with accurate row estimates. Risk boundary: an index that helps one query can degrade write throughput and bloat storage; record the write impact and escalate index changes on hot OLTP tables.
